attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for her first antenatal visit at eight weeks of pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. This is her first pregnancy. She reports she has not been taking any supplements before conception. Her sister's previous pregnancy was affected by anencephaly, and she is concerned about her own risk. Her last menstrual period was eight weeks ago, and she has had a positive home pregnancy test.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of anencephaly if detected in this pregnancy?
attributes.explanation:
Anencephaly is a severe form of neural tube defect characterised by the absence of a major portion of the brain, skull, and scalp. Understanding its embryological basis is crucial for prevention strategies, particularly in cases with family history.

Failed neural tube closure during early embryonic development (days 23-26 post-conception) is the primary mechanism leading to anencephaly. The neural tube forms the primitive structure that develops into the brain and spinal cord. Failure of closure at the cranial end results in anencephaly. While having a sibling with anencephaly increases the risk approximately 3-5%, most cases are sporadic rather than inherited.

Chromosomal abnormality is rarely associated with isolated neural tube defects. While chromosomal abnormalities can cause various congenital malformations, they are not the primary mechanism for anencephaly, even in cases with family history.

Vascular disruption typically causes defects later in fetal development after the neural tube has closed. It is more commonly associated with limb reduction defects or gastroschisis rather than neural tube defects.

Amniotic band disruption occurs after neural tube closure and typically causes asymmetric defects. It cannot explain the systematic failure of neural tube closure seen in anencephaly.

Teratogenic medication exposure, while capable of causing various birth defects, is not the primary mechanism for anencephaly. However, certain medications (such as anticonvulsants) may increase the risk of neural tube defects.

Prevention of neural tube defects primarily focuses on adequate folic acid supplementation (400 micrograms daily) before conception and during early pregnancy. Women with a family history of neural tube defects are typically recommended higher doses of folic acid (4-5 mg daily) in the periconceptional period.
learningPoints: Anencephaly occurs due to failure of neural tube closure in early embryonic development, typically between days 23-26 of pregnancy.
Folic acid supplementation before conception and in early pregnancy significantly reduces the risk of neural tube defects.
First trimester ultrasound can reliably detect anencephaly through visualization of absent cranial vault.
Most cases of anencephaly are sporadic rather than inherited, though family history increases risk.
references: Anencephaly. MedlinePlus. Bethesda (MD): U.S. National Library of Medicine; [updated 2021 Jul 5; cited 2024 Jun 16]. Available from: https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/anencephaly/#causes
Cook RJ, Erdman JN, Hevia M, Dickens BM. Prenatal management of anencephaly. Int J Gynaecol Obstet. 2008 Sep;102(3):304-8. doi: 10.1016/j.ijgo.2008.05.002. Epub 2008 Jul 7. PMID: 18603246.

Johnson SP, Sebire NJ, Snijders RJ, Tunkel S, Nicolaides KH. Ultrasound screening for anencephaly at 10-14 weeks of gestation. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol. 1997 Jan;9(1):14-6. doi: 10.1046/j.1469-0705.1997.09010014.x. PMID: 9060123.
